## 6. High-Level Design (one requirement at a time)

Color convention used in every diagram: blue = clients/agents, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Ingest logs from thousands of services"

The firehose. The whole art is decoupling the producers (100K hosts) from the index so a burst doesn't crash anything downstream.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Host agent | A small daemon on every host that tails log files and ships batches | Needs a local buffer + back-pressure so it doesn't OOM the host during a stall | Not direct app→index writes (couples every app to ES; one slow index stalls all apps) | Agent can drop on-host under extreme local backlog |
| Ingest gateway | Stateless service that authenticates batches and writes to Kafka | Needs a thin, horizontally scalable front door that does auth + quota + sampling | Not writing to ES directly (no buffer; index becomes the bottleneck at 5M/s) | One extra hop of latency |
| Kafka | A durable, ordered log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | Absorbs the 5x burst, decouples producers from indexers, replays on failure | Not SQS/RabbitMQ (lower sustained throughput, weaker replay); not in-memory queue (loses data on crash) | Operational complexity of running brokers |
| Indexer workers | Stateless consumers that read Kafka, transform, and bulk-write to ES | Lets us scale index throughput independently and apply sampling at one place | Not Logstash-as-monolith (heavier, harder to scale the hot path) | Another fleet to operate |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Every host runs an agent that tails logs and ships batches — I deliberately don't let apps write to the index directly, because then one slow index stalls every app. The agent hits a stateless ingest gateway that does auth, per-service quota, and sampling, then writes into Kafka — a durable ordered log that soaks up the burst. Indexer workers read off Kafka at their own pace and bulk-write into the search index. The trade-off is more moving parts, but the payoff is that the firehose and the index are completely decoupled."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Host agent** — a lightweight program on each server that collects log lines and forwards them. Here it gives us an on-host buffer so a network blip doesn't lose logs.
> **Kafka** — a durable append-only log that many producers write to and many consumers read from at their own speed. Here it buffers the 25M/s burst so the index never sees the spike directly.
> **Back-pressure** — the mechanism by which a slow downstream tells an upstream to slow down. Here the gateway's 202-with-dropped-count tells agents they're being sampled.
> **Bulk write** — sending many documents to the index in one request instead of one-at-a-time. Here it's the only way to hit millions of docs/s.

**DIAGRAM — Ingest firehose**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host agents x100K]
    A -->|"1 POST batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    GW -->|"2 auth and quota"| GW
    GW -->|"3 produce events"| K[[Kafka log buffer]]
    K -->|"4 consume at own pace"| IX[Indexer workers]
    IX -->|"5 bulk index"| ES[(Search index)]
    IX -->|"6 emit raw"| OBJ[(Cold object store)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class A client
    class GW,IX svc
    class K queue
    class ES,OBJ db
```
↳ new components: agent, gateway, Kafka, indexer, search index, cold store.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Agent POSTs a batch** of ~1000 events to the gateway. *Why the agent:* it owns the on-host buffer and back-pressure. *State:* events leave the host. *What goes wrong:* network stall → agent buffers locally, then drops debug-level first if the buffer fills.
2. **Gateway authenticates and applies quota/sampling.** *Why here:* one central place to enforce per-service limits and shed load. *State:* batch is admitted (maybe partially). *What goes wrong:* overload → gateway samples and returns a dropped count.
3. **Gateway produces to Kafka,** partitioned by service. *Why Kafka:* durable buffer that absorbs bursts. *State:* events are now durably persisted (replicated x3). *What goes wrong:* a broker dies → another replica serves; producer retries are idempotent on batch_id.
4. **Indexer workers consume** at their own throughput. *Why decoupled:* the index can lag without back-pressuring producers. *State:* events in flight to the index. *What goes wrong:* indexer lag grows → we add workers or sample harder; Kafka holds the backlog.
5. **Workers bulk-index into the search index.** *Why bulk:* only way to hit millions of docs/s. *State:* events become searchable (~5s lag). *What goes wrong:* index rejects writes (full) → workers pause, Kafka buffers, alarms fire.
6. **Workers also tee raw events to cold object storage** (compressed). *Why:* cheap durable archive independent of the index for replay and compliance. *State:* permanent copy exists. *What goes wrong:* object store slow → buffered async, never blocks the index.

> 💬 "The one insight: I never let an app talk to the index. Kafka is the shock absorber between a firehose that bursts 5x and an index that has a fixed write ceiling."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Agents ship batches to a stateless gateway that auths and samples, then drops everything into Kafka — a durable log that buffers the burst. Indexer workers pull from Kafka at whatever rate they can sustain and bulk-write into the search index, and tee a compressed copy to cold object storage. The decision that makes this work is decoupling: Kafka means a five-times burst inflates the queue, not the index, and the index never has to scale to peak."

### 6.2 "Search and filter logs"

Now the read path — tiny in QPS, but it must search an inverted index that's being written at 5M docs/s.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search index (ES/OpenSearch) | A search engine that pre-builds an inverted index — a map of every word to the events containing it | Need ranked full-text + field filters over trillions of rows in <1s; only an inverted index can | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row — unusable at this scale); not grep over files (no index, minutes per query) | Index lags raw by ~5s; expensive to store |
| Query service | Stateless API that fans a query across time-relevant index shards and merges results | Routes to only the shards covering the time window, enforces authz | Not querying every shard always (wasteful — most queries are last 15 min) | Slightly more routing logic |
| Query cache | A short-TTL cache of recent query results | Dashboard + repeated queries are highly cacheable | Not caching nothing (dashboards would storm the index) | Stale up to the TTL (fine for logs) |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Search runs on an inverted index — think of the index at the back of a book mapping each word to the pages it appears on, except here words map to log events. I picked a search engine like Elasticsearch because no relational database can do ranked full-text over trillions of rows in under a second. A stateless query service fans the query only across the shards covering the requested time window — most queries are 'last fifteen minutes', so it touches a tiny slice. A short-TTL cache absorbs repeated and dashboard queries."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Inverted index** — a precomputed map from each term to the list of documents containing it, so search is a lookup, not a scan. Here it turns full-text search over trillions of logs into milliseconds.
> **Shard** — one horizontal slice of the index living on one node. Here we shard by time so old data can be moved to cheaper nodes or dropped wholesale.
> **Query fan-out** — sending one query to many shards and merging the answers. Here it's scoped to only the shards in the time window.

**DIAGRAM — Search read path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Engineer browser]
    U -->|"1 GET search"| QS[Query service]
    QS -->|"2 check cache"| QC[(Query cache)]
    QC -.->|"3 miss"| QS
    QS -->|"4 fan to time shards"| ES[(Search index)]
    ES -->|"5 ranked hits"| QS
    QS -->|"6 cache and return"| U
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class U client
    class QS svc
    class QC,ES db
```
↳ reuses existing: search index (from §6.1).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Engineer issues a search.** *Why query service:* it owns authz + routing. *State:* request enters. *What goes wrong:* unauthorized service → 403 before any index hit.
2. **Query service checks the cache.** *Why:* dashboards and repeats are common. *State:* cache lookup. *What goes wrong:* cache down → bypass to index, slower but correct.
3. **On miss, proceed to the index.** *State:* will hit ES.
4. **Fan out only to shards covering the time window.** *Why:* avoids touching cold/old shards. *State:* parallel shard queries. *What goes wrong:* one slow shard → partial results + a "results may be incomplete" flag rather than hanging.
5. **Index returns ranked hits.** *State:* merged result set.
6. **Cache and return with a cursor.** *State:* cached for 30s; client can page. *What goes wrong:* huge result → we cap and paginate via cursor.

> 💬 "Search is only ~20 uncached queries a second — laughably small — so the read tier is almost free. The cost is all in building the index, not querying it."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "An engineer searches, the query service checks a short cache, and on a miss fans out only to the index shards covering the time window — because almost every query is 'last fifteen minutes'. Results come back ranked and get cached for thirty seconds. The key point is that reads are trivial here — twenty uncached queries a second — so I spend nothing scaling the read path; the expense is keeping the inverted index built and fresh."

### 6.3 "Alert on error spikes"

The trap here is alerting by scanning raw logs. We pre-aggregate counts as data streams, then evaluate cheap rules against the counts.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream aggregator (Flink) | A stream processor that reads Kafka and maintains running per-service-per-minute error counts | Need continuous aggregation at 5M/s — must compute on the stream, not on read | Not periodic ES count queries (10K rules x scans = a self-DDoS); not cron over raw logs | Approximate counts during late-arriving data |
| Aggregate store | A small fast store holding the rollup counters and time-series | Tiny vs raw logs; powers both alerts and dashboards | Not raw-log scans (orders of magnitude more expensive) | Rollups are lossy by design (counts, not lines) |
| Alert evaluator | Service that checks the ~10K rules against the rollups each interval | Cheap because it reads counters, not logs | Not evaluating inside each query (couples alerting to UI) | Slight detection delay (~60-90s) |
| Notifier | Sends pages/Slack via external systems | Decouples detection from delivery + dedup | Not paging directly from the evaluator (retries/dedup get messy) | Extra hop |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Alerting is where people get it wrong — they try to count errors by querying the index every minute, and with ten thousand rules that's a self-inflicted denial of service. Instead I use a stream processor, Flink — software that reads the Kafka stream and keeps a live running count of errors per service per minute. Those tiny counters land in an aggregate store. The alert evaluator then just compares ten thousand rules against those counters, which is dirt cheap, and hands breaches to a notifier that dedupes and pages."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink** — a stream-processing engine that computes running aggregates over an event stream in real time. Here it counts errors per service per minute as logs flow by, so alerting never scans raw data.
> **Windowed aggregation** — grouping events into time buckets (e.g. per-minute) and computing a value per bucket. Here it's the per-service error count that rules check.
> **Rollup** — a small precomputed summary that replaces scanning the raw data. Here it shrinks trillions of logs to a few counters per service.

**DIAGRAM — Alerting pipeline**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka log buffer]]
    K -->|"1 consume stream"| FL[Stream aggregator]
    FL -->|"2 write rollups"| AG[(Aggregate store)]
    EV[Alert evaluator]
    EV -->|"3 read counters"| AG
    EV -->|"4 breach detected"| NT[Notifier]
    NT -->|"5 page or message"| EXT[PagerDuty Slack]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#2e1a3a,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
    class FL,EV,NT svc
    class K queue
    class AG db
    class EXT ext
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Aggregator consumes the same Kafka stream** the indexers use. *Why same stream:* one source of truth, no extra read load. *State:* events flow through. *What goes wrong:* aggregator lag → counts trail real-time; bounded by Kafka retention.
2. **It writes per-minute rollups** to the aggregate store. *State:* counters updated. *What goes wrong:* late-arriving events → handled with a small grace window; very late events update next bucket.
3. **Evaluator reads counters** every interval. *Why:* cheap reads. *State:* rule checks run. *What goes wrong:* evaluator down → standby instance takes over; rules re-evaluate from stored counters.
4. **A breach is detected.** *State:* alert state transitions to FIRING. *What goes wrong:* flapping → require N consecutive breaches before paging.
5. **Notifier pages**, deduping repeats. *State:* on-call paged. *What goes wrong:* PagerDuty down → fall back to Slack/email, queue for retry.

> 💬 "The whole alerting cost trick: I count errors once as they stream by, so ten thousand rules read counters, not logs."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Alerting reads the same Kafka stream as indexing, but instead of storing logs it keeps running per-minute error counts in a tiny aggregate store. The evaluator compares ten thousand rules against those counters every interval — cheap, because it's reading numbers, not scanning logs — and on a sustained breach it hands off to a notifier that dedupes and pages. The decision is pre-aggregation: I compute the counts once at write time rather than ten thousand times at read time."

### 6.4 "Dashboards"

Dashboards reuse the alerting rollups — no new datastore, just read the aggregate store.

↳ builds on §6.3 diagram — no new components. The dashboard service reads the **aggregate store** with a 30s cache, exactly the rollups the alert evaluator uses. The only new edge: 2,000 panels → dashboard service → aggregate store (cached). This is why dashboards can't storm the index: they never touch it.

> 💬 "Dashboards and alerts share the same counters — build the rollup once, use it twice."

### 6.5 "Tiered retention and lifecycle"

Reuses the cold object store and search index; adds a lifecycle manager that ages shards down tiers and drops expired partitions.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lifecycle manager | A scheduler that moves time-shards hot→warm→cold and drops expired ones | At ~260 TB/day, retention is the cost lever; must reclaim space in O(1) | Not row-by-row DELETE (churns the index, never reclaims cleanly) | Old data slower to query (warm/cold) |
| Cold object store (S3/Glacier) | Cheap durable storage for compressed raw logs | ~25x cheaper than hot; durable; rehydratable for rare deep queries | Not keeping everything in ES (would be ~$2M/mo just hot) | Cold queries take minutes (rehydrate) |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Retention is a cost decision dressed as an engineering one. A lifecycle manager moves whole time-based index shards from hot SSD nodes to cheaper warm nodes after a week, archives the compressed raw to Glacier-class object storage, and then drops entire expired partitions in one operation. I never delete logs row by row — at this volume that churns the index and never reclaims space. Cold queries are rare and slow, and that's the deliberate trade-off."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Time-partitioning** — splitting data into shards by time window (e.g. one per hour). Here it lets us drop a whole expired window instantly.
> **Partition drop** — deleting an entire partition as one cheap metadata operation. Here it reclaims petabytes in O(1) instead of churning through rows.
> **Tiering** — moving data across hot/warm/cold storage by age and access frequency. Here it's the difference between ~$420K and several million a month.

**DIAGRAM — Lifecycle tiering**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LC[Lifecycle manager]
    ES[(Search index hot)]
    WARM[(Warm index nodes)]
    OBJ[(Cold object store)]
    LC -->|"1 age week-old shards"| WARM
    ES -->|"2 move shard"| WARM
    LC -->|"3 archive raw"| OBJ
    LC -->|"4 drop expired partition"| ES
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class LC svc
    class ES,WARM,OBJ db
```
↳ reuses existing: search index, cold object store (from §6.1).

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Lifecycle manager identifies week-old shards.** *Why:* hot tier is expensive SSD. *State:* shards flagged. *What goes wrong:* misconfigured policy → guardrail blocks dropping anything under min-retention.
2. **Shard moves to warm nodes** (cheaper disk, one replica). *State:* still queryable, slower. *What goes wrong:* move fails → shard stays hot, alarmed.
3. **Raw archived to cold object store** compressed. *State:* permanent cheap copy. *What goes wrong:* already teed at ingest, so this is idempotent.
4. **Expired partition dropped** as one operation. *State:* space reclaimed instantly. *What goes wrong:* dropping live data → partitions are immutable + below min-retention check.

> 💬 "The cost story lives here: drop whole partitions, never rows, and let Glacier hold the long tail."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A lifecycle manager ages index shards from hot SSD to warm disk after a week, keeps a compressed copy in cold object storage, and drops entire expired partitions in one cheap operation. The decision is partition-drop over row-delete — at a quarter petabyte a day, anything else churns the index and bleeds money. Cold data is slow to query, and we accept that because nobody interactively searches last March."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LogEvent | event_id (PK), ts, **service (shard)**, level, message, trace_id, fields(json) | Search index + cold object store | by service + time window | Eventual (~5s lag) |
| Service | service_id (PK), team_id, quota_eps | Relational (Postgres) | service_id | Strong |
| AlertRule | rule_id (PK), target, condition, notify, created_by | Relational (Postgres) | rule_id | Strong |
| Aggregate | (service, level, minute) composite PK | Aggregate store (time-series/Redis+TSDB) | service + time | Eventual (~10s) |
| RetentionPolicy | policy_id (PK), scope, hot_days, warm_days, cold_days | Relational (Postgres) | policy_id | Strong |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Search index (Elasticsearch / OpenSearch) — used for: LogEvent (hot + warm)**
1. **What it is:** a search engine that builds an inverted index — a precomputed map from every word/field-value to the events containing it — so a full-text query is a lookup, not a scan.
2. **Why we picked it:** we need ranked full-text + structured-field search over trillions of immutable rows returning in <1s. Only an inverted index does that; the time-sharded layout also makes tiering and partition-drop trivial.
3. **Considered instead:**
   - **PostgreSQL full-text** — fine at millions of rows, collapses at trillions; no horizontal index sharding for our volume.
   - **ClickHouse** — superb for aggregations/scans and cheaper storage, but weaker ad-hoc full-text ranking; a strong alternative I'd revisit if search were mostly analytical, not keyword.
   - **Hand-rolled inverted index** — months of work, no relevance ranking, reinventing Lucene.
4. **Trade-off:** expensive to store (index overhead) and lags raw by ~5s; mitigated by tiering and sampling.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For search I'm using an Elasticsearch-style inverted index because nothing else does sub-second full-text over trillions of logs. I considered ClickHouse — it's cheaper and great at aggregations — and I'd switch if queries were mostly analytical, but for keyword search ES wins. The cost is index storage, which I control with tiering."

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: the ingest buffer (transient LogEvent stream)**
1. **What it is:** a durable, replicated, append-only log that producers write to and many consumers read from independently at their own pace.
2. **Why we picked it:** it's the shock absorber — it soaks the 25M/s burst, decouples agents from the index, lets indexers and the aggregator consume the same stream, and replays on failure.
3. **Considered instead:**
   - **SQS/Kinesis** — managed and simpler, but lower sustained throughput per cost and weaker multi-consumer replay at this scale.
   - **RabbitMQ** — great for routing, not for a multi-GB/s firehose with replay.
4. **Trade-off:** brokers are real infrastructure to operate; we accept that for the durability + replay.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Kafka is the buffer between a firehose that bursts five-x and an index with a fixed write ceiling. I looked at Kinesis for less ops burden but the throughput-per-dollar and replay story favored Kafka at three gigabytes a second."

**🗄️ Cold object store (S3 + Glacier) — used for: raw LogEvent archive**
1. **What it is:** cheap, massively durable blob storage for compressed raw logs, ~25x cheaper than hot SSD.
2. **Why we picked it:** at 19 PB/year, only object storage is affordable for long-tail compliance retention; it's 11-nines durable and rehydratable for rare deep queries.
3. **Considered instead:** keeping everything in ES (~$2M+/mo, absurd); a self-run HDFS cluster (more ops, less durable than S3).
4. **Trade-off:** cold queries require rehydration (minutes), which is fine because deep-history search is rare.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Anything older than a month goes to Glacier-class storage compressed — twenty-five times cheaper. The trade-off is a slow rehydrate for the rare query into last quarter, and I'm happy to pay that in seconds-of-wait to save millions."

**🗄️ Aggregate store (time-series DB + Redis) — used for: Aggregate rollups**
1. **What it is:** a small fast store of per-service-per-minute counters, with hot recent windows in Redis and history in a time-series DB.
2. **Why we picked it:** alerts and dashboards must read counts in milliseconds; the data is tiny (counters, not logs).
3. **Considered instead:** querying ES for counts (self-DDoS at 10K rules); Postgres (fine but slower for time-series rollups at scale).
4. **Trade-off:** rollups are lossy summaries by design — you can't drill from a counter to the exact lines without going to the index.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Alerts and dashboards read tiny per-minute counters from a time-series store, not raw logs. That's the only way ten thousand rules and two thousand panels stay cheap."

**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Service, AlertRule, RetentionPolicy (control plane)**
1. **What it is:** a relational database with strong transactions for the small, structured config data.
2. **Why we picked it:** rule/service/policy config is low-volume, relational, and needs strong consistency and authz joins.
3. **Considered instead:** stuffing config into ES (wrong tool — config isn't searchable logs).
4. **Trade-off:** none meaningful at this volume — it's a small control-plane DB.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The control plane — services, alert rules, retention policies — is just Postgres. It's small, relational, and needs strong consistency, so I don't overthink it."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest a log | Kafka → index | Eventual (~5s) | Decoupling beats instant search; ack on durable buffer |
| Create alert rule | Postgres | Strong | Config correctness; must not lose a rule |
| Read rollup for alert | Aggregate store | Eventual (~10s) | Counts trail real-time slightly; acceptable for 60s detection |
| Search logs | Search index | Eventual | A 5s-old log being absent is fine |
| Read-your-own-writes (just-logged event) | index | NOT guaranteed instantly | Within ~5s lag; UI shows "indexing…" hint; tail-from-Kafka option for live tail |

**PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE (largest table: LogEvent)**

At ~260 TB/day raw, the hot index alone is ~2 PB for 7 days (the $200K/mo line from §3). Retention caps it: **7 days hot, ~30 days warm, 365 days cold in Glacier, then expire.** Reclamation is **partition-drop** on time-sharded indices — dropping a whole day's shard is one O(1) metadata operation, never a row-by-row DELETE (which churns the inverted index and never cleanly reclaims SSD). Cold data lives compressed in object storage and is rehydrated only for rare deep queries. Without tiering, hot storage grows unbounded toward millions of dollars a month.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The core stores are an Elasticsearch-style index for hot and warm searchable logs, Kafka as the durable buffer in front of it, and Glacier-class object storage for the cheap long tail. Alerts and dashboards run off a tiny time-series aggregate store, not the raw index, and the control plane is plain Postgres. The one I'd flag: I considered ClickHouse instead of ES and would switch if the workload were mostly analytical rather than keyword search. And the lifecycle answer for the giant log table is partition-drop on time shards — instant reclamation, never row deletes."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the binding number — 5M events/s in, 70K reads out, and a fixed index write ceiling — and reason forward from "what do I buffer and what do I drop."

### How do we survive the ingestion firehose and its 5x bursts?

**#### Bad: Agents write directly to the index**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every app sends its logs straight into the search engine.
- **Approach:** each service POSTs log lines to Elasticsearch as they happen.
- **Why people try this:** it's the obvious, fewest-moving-parts design.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the index has a fixed write ceiling (~15K docs/s/node). At 5M/s you'd need ~330 nodes always, and a 5x burst to 25M/s instantly overruns them — index write queues fill, rejections cascade, and worse, the *apps themselves* slow down because they're blocked writing logs during an incident. The tool fails exactly when needed.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any burst, and the coupling of app health to index health.

**#### Good: Buffer in Kafka, index asynchronously**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad coupled producers to the index, so a burst or slow index stalled the apps. Good inserts a durable buffer so producers never wait on the index.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** logs go into a big durable waiting line first; the index drains the line at its own pace.
- **Approach:** agents → gateway → Kafka (a durable replicated log). Indexer workers consume and bulk-write. A 5x burst inflates the *queue depth*, not the index load; Kafka holds the backlog (sized for ~hours of retention) and indexers catch up after the spike.
- **Why reasonable:** decoupling is the standard firehose pattern.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a *sustained* (not transient) overload — say a bad deploy logging at 10x for an hour — grows Kafka lag unboundedly. Searchable lag blows past 5s to minutes; eventually Kafka retention is exceeded and you lose the *oldest* data silently, which may be the most important.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agents] -->|"produce burst"| K[[Kafka buffer]]
    K -->|"drain at own pace"| IX[Indexer workers]
    IX -->|"bulk index"| ES[(Search index)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class A,IX svc
    class K queue
    class ES db
```
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** sustained overload that outpaces both indexers and Kafka retention — we need to *choose* what to drop, not lose data randomly.

**#### Great: Tiered sampling + back-pressure under sustained overload**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good would, under sustained overload, silently drop the *oldest* (often most-relevant) data when Kafka retention is exceeded. Great makes dropping a *deliberate, prioritized* decision at the gateway.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when we're drowning, we throw away the boring logs first and keep the errors, on purpose, and tell the agent we did.
- **Approach:** the gateway watches **consumer lag** (how far behind indexers are) and **Kafka queue depth**. Load-shedding order: (1) shed debug/info logs first via head-sampling (keep 1-in-N), (2) keep WARN at reduced rate, (3) **never drop ERROR/FATAL**. The 202 response carries `dropped` counts so agents apply their own back-pressure. Kafka is the bounded buffer — it spills nothing to disk beyond retention; we drop at the gateway *before* Kafka so the buffer protects the high-value path.
- **🔢 Decision-forcing math:** headroom-sized fleet ~500 ES nodes x 15K = ~7.5M docs/s sustainable. Average is 5M/s (fits), peak 25M/s does NOT. So at >7.5M/s sustained we sample: keeping 100% of ~10% error logs + sampling the 90% info logs at 1-in-5 brings 25M/s down to ~2.5M error + ~4.5M sampled = ~7M/s — back under ceiling, with zero error loss.
- **✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Transient 5x burst | Kafka absorbs, indexers catch up, no drops |
| Sustained 5x for an hour | Gateway samples info/debug, keeps all errors, lag stays bounded |
| Indexer fleet loses 30% | Lag rises → sampling kicks in earlier → no cascade |
| Kafka broker dies | Replica serves; producer retries idempotent on batch_id |
| Gateway overloaded | Returns 429 + Retry-After; agents buffer locally then drop debug |
| Agent can't reach gateway | On-host buffer, then drop lowest severity first |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Agents] -->|"batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    GW -->|"watch lag"| LAG[Lag signal]
    LAG -->|"over ceiling"| GW
    GW -->|"keep errors drop info"| K[[Kafka buffer]]
    GW -.->|"202 with dropped count"| A
    K --> IX[Indexer workers]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    class A client
    class GW,IX,LAG svc
    class K queue
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The firehose answer is buffer-then-sample. Everything goes through Kafka so a transient burst inflates the queue, not the index. But for a sustained overload — a bad deploy logging ten-x for an hour — I shed deliberately at the gateway: drop debug and info first, sample warnings, and never, ever drop errors and fatals, because those are why the tool exists. The gateway watches indexer lag and tells agents they're being sampled via the response, so back-pressure is honest, not silent."

> 🧠 **If they ask "isn't dropping logs dangerous?":** "Only if they're audit logs — and I locked that assumption out front. For an ops tool, a sampled info log during a storm is a fine trade for never dropping an error and never crashing the pipe."

### How do we build the inverted index at 5M docs/s and keep it queryable?

**#### Good: One big index, write and read on the same nodes**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** one giant searchable pile that you both write into and read from.
- **Approach:** all logs in one large time-rolled index; queries hit it directly.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** writes (5M/s) and queries compete for the same CPU and disk on the same nodes; a heavy query during heavy ingest causes both to suffer. And one index can't be tiered granularly — you can't cheaply move "last week" off SSD.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** write/read contention + inability to tier.

**#### Great: Time-sharded indices with hot-warm-cold node roles**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good mixed write-heavy recent data and read-heavy old data on the same nodes, and couldn't tier. Great shards by time and assigns node roles by age.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** split the index into time slices — today's slice lives on fast machines that take the writes, older slices live on cheaper machines, and queries only touch the slices in your time range.
- **Approach:** roll a new index every hour/day; recent (write-active) shards sit on **hot nodes** (fast NVMe, more CPU); aged shards move to **warm nodes** (cheap disk, one replica, read-only). Queries fan out only to shards overlapping the requested window — most are "last 15 min" so they touch one hot shard. Dropping old data = drop the whole time-shard (the §7 partition-drop).
- **🔢 Decision-forcing math:** with ~90% of queries on the last hour, hot nodes serve nearly all reads against ~1/168th of the week's data — so the hot tier stays small and fast while warm holds the bulk cheaply.
- **✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Heavy query during heavy ingest | Query hits warm shards, ingest hits hot shards — no contention |
| Hot node dies | Replica promoted; recent data has 2 replicas |
| Warm node dies | One replica → brief reindex from cold/Kafka replay |
| Query spans 30 days | Fans to warm shards, slower but bounded; UI warns wide window |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QS[Query service]
    QS -->|"last 15 min"| HOT[(Hot shards today)]
    QS -->|"wide window"| WARM[(Warm shards aged)]
    IX[Indexer workers] -->|"write only"| HOT
    LC[Lifecycle manager] -->|"age shard"| WARM
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class QS,IX,LC svc
    class HOT,WARM db
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I shard the index by time. Writes only ever land on today's hot shards on fast machines, while older shards age onto cheap warm nodes that are read-only. Because almost every query is 'what happened in the last fifteen minutes', reads barely touch the same nodes that ingest is hammering — that kills the write-versus-read contention. And aging out data is just dropping a whole time-shard, which is instant."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not ClickHouse for cheaper storage?":** "If queries were mostly aggregations I'd seriously consider it — it's cheaper per byte. But for ad-hoc full-text keyword search with ranking, the inverted index wins; I'd benchmark both before committing real money."

### How do we alert on error spikes without scanning raw logs?

**#### Bad: Cron queries the index for counts every minute**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every minute, ask the search engine "how many errors?" for every rule.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** 10K rules x a count query each = ~170 heavy aggregation queries/s slamming the index — a self-inflicted DDoS that competes with ingest and real searches. Detection also lags by the query cost.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the index can't be both the log store and the alerting engine.

**#### Great: Stream pre-aggregation with windowed counters**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad recomputed counts from raw logs 170 times a second. Great computes each count exactly once as the data streams by.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a live running tally of errors per service per minute as logs flow through, then alerts just read the tally.
- **Approach:** a stream processor (Flink) consumes the Kafka stream and maintains windowed counters — total + error count per service per minute — written to the aggregate store. The evaluator reads counters and checks rules. Late/out-of-order events get a small grace window. Rules require **N consecutive breaches** to avoid flapping; the notifier dedupes so one incident is one page.
- **🔢 Decision-forcing math:** evaluating 10K rules against in-memory counters is ~170 cheap key-reads/s vs ~170 trillion-row aggregations/s — roughly a million-fold reduction in cost.
- **✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Aggregator lags | Counts trail real-time; detection delay grows but bounded by Kafka |
| Aggregator crashes | Restart from Kafka offset + checkpoint; counters rebuilt |
| Late events | Grace window includes them; very late → next bucket |
| Flapping metric | N-consecutive-breach rule suppresses noise |
| Duplicate events (retry) | Dedup on event_id so counts aren't inflated |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka buffer]] -->|"stream"| FL[Stream aggregator]
    FL -->|"per-minute counts"| AG[(Aggregate store)]
    EV[Alert evaluator] -->|"read counters"| AG
    EV -->|"N breaches"| NT[Notifier]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class FL,EV,NT svc
    class K queue
    class AG db
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Alerting never touches raw logs. A stream processor keeps live per-minute error counts as events flow through Kafka, and the evaluator compares ten thousand rules against those counters — a million times cheaper than re-querying the index. I require a few consecutive breaches before paging to kill flapping, and dedupe so one incident is one page, not a hundred."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what about a brand-new error type with no rule?":** "Counters are per-service-per-level, so a spike in ERROR rate for any service trips the generic per-service error-rate rule even if no one wrote a specific rule for that message."

### How do we keep the storage bill from exploding (the cost deep dive)?

**#### Good: Keep everything in the hot index for 90 days**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** leave all logs in the fast search engine for three months.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** ~285 TB/day indexed x 90 days x $0.10/GB ≈ **$2.5M/mo** just for hot storage — economically absurd for data nobody searches after a week.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** cost.

**#### Great: Three-tier lifecycle + sampling-at-source**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good paid hot-tier prices for cold-access data. Great matches storage cost to access frequency and shrinks the input.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a week on fast storage, a month on cheap disk, a year in deep archive, and don't even index the boring logs at full fidelity.
- **Approach:** (1) **tier** hot→warm→cold by age with partition-drop expiry; (2) **sample** info/debug at the gateway so we index ~7M/s not 25M/s; (3) **compress** raw to zstd (~5:1) before cold archive. Cold is rehydrate-on-demand.
- **🔢 Decision-forcing math:** tiering takes the all-hot $2.5M/mo down to ~$420K/mo storage (hot $200K + warm $148K + cold $76K). Unit economics: **~$0.09 per million events, ~$90 per billion** — the number to drive down. Halving info-log volume via sampling directly halves the index storage line.
- **✅ Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Need a query into last quarter | Rehydrate cold shard (minutes), then search |
| Compliance demands longer retention | Extend cold tier only (cheap); hot/warm unchanged |
| Sampling drops a needed info log | Errors always kept; raw uncompressed copy still in cold for replay |
| Cost spikes from a noisy service | Per-service quota caps it; bill it back to the team |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Incoming logs] -->|"sample info drop"| HOT[(Hot 7d)]
    HOT -->|"age 7d"| WARM[(Warm 30d)]
    WARM -->|"age 30d"| COLD[(Cold 365d Glacier)]
    COLD -->|"expire"| DEL[Dropped]
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    class HOT,WARM,COLD db
    class IN,DEL svc
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage is the cost game. All-hot for ninety days would be about two and a half million a month — insane. So three tiers: a week hot, a month warm, a year in Glacier, then expire by dropping whole partitions. That plus sampling the boring logs gets us to around four hundred grand. The number I actually manage is cost-per-million-events — about nine cents — and the biggest lever on it is how aggressively I sample info logs."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what's the single biggest cost optimization?":** "Sampling at source. Every info log I don't index saves index CPU, hot storage, and replicas all at once — it's the only lever that hits compute and storage together."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets.** Ingest path targets **99.9% ("three nines" = ~8.7 hours/year down)**; query path **99.5% (~1.8 days/year)** — query can blink, ingest mostly can't. Mechanisms: Kafka replication factor 3 across **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically)*; stateless gateways/indexers behind a load balancer auto-scale and auto-replace; the index runs ≥2 replicas for hot shards.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER SEES) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest gateway | New logs can't enter | Agents buffer on-host, then drop debug first; users briefly see "indexing delayed" | Auto-scale / replace; agents flush buffer |
| Kafka | Buffer unavailable | Gateway returns 429; agents buffer locally; brief gap in newest logs | Replica/partition leader election; producers retry |
| Indexer workers | Index falls behind | Search lag rises from 5s toward minutes; a banner says "results may be delayed" | Add workers; Kafka replay |
| Search index (hot) | Recent search down | Users can still see dashboards/alerts (those use rollups), but interactive search fails | Replica promote; rebuild shard from Kafka/cold |
| Stream aggregator | Alerts/dashboards stale | Alerts may delay; dashboards freeze at last value with a "stale" marker | Restart from Kafka offset + checkpoint |
| Aggregate store | Alert evaluation blind | Evaluator holds last state; new breaches missed briefly — escalate to a heartbeat alarm | Failover replica |
| Cold object store | Deep-history queries fail | Recent search/alerts unaffected; "archive temporarily unavailable" | S3 is 11-nines; transient retry |

**9C — RPO / RTO.** **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we can lose if it crashes right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until we're working again.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buffered logs (in Kafka) | ~0 | minutes | RF3 multi-AZ replication; replay on restart |
| In-flight logs (pre-Kafka) | seconds (best-effort) | n/a | Agent on-host buffer; acceptable per assumption 2 |
| Search index | rebuildable (minutes-hours lag OK) | ~1 hour | Reindex from cold archive / Kafka replay |
| Alert rules / config | ~0 | minutes | Postgres synchronous replica + PITR backups |
| Aggregate rollups | seconds | minutes | Recompute from Kafka offset |

**9D — Cost.** Top 3 drivers: **(1) ES compute ~$600K/mo** — the index fleet is the single biggest line because indexing at millions of docs/s burns CPU and NVMe; **(2) storage ~$420K/mo** — petabytes across tiers; **(3) ingest workers ~$125K/mo**. Total **~$1.2M/mo**, unit economics **~$0.09/1M events**. Biggest single optimization: **sample info/debug at source** — it cuts index CPU, hot storage, and replicas simultaneously, the only lever that hits compute and storage together.

**Back-pressure (hottest async path = Kafka ingest):** the gateway watches consumer lag + queue depth; over threshold it sheds in order debug→info→warn, never errors, and returns 429 + Retry-After when truly saturated. Kafka is bounded by retention; we drop *before* Kafka, not by overflowing it. **Observability:** golden signals — ingest EPS (traffic), index lag (latency), drop rate (errors), Kafka/ES disk (saturation); a burn-rate alert pages if drop rate exceeds budget; a **trace_id** propagates agent→gateway→Kafka→indexer so one event is followable across the async hop. **Rate-limiting:** per-agent and per-service token-bucket quotas at the gateway (the limiter state is itself a global coordinator, kept in Redis); over quota → 429 + Retry-After.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the gateway is down, agents buffer on the host and you see a short 'indexing delayed' banner — no crash. When indexers fall behind, search just gets staler and we say so. The one thing I protect above all is error logs through the buffer — Kafka's three-way replication means once a log is buffered, an AZ can burn down and we don't lose it. And the bill is dominated by the index fleet, so the lever I pull first is sampling."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Best-effort sampled ingest vs guaranteed exactly-once.**
1. **Chose & why:** sampling-allowed ingest — cheaper, survives bursts, fits an ops tool.
2. **Gave up:** a guarantee that every single log is captured.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like a security camera that drops a few frames when overwhelmed but never misses the important motion.
4. **When this reverses:** if logs become legally required (audit/compliance) — then a separate exactly-once WORM path, no shedding, ~2x cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I chose best-effort because it's an ops tool; if any of this is a legal audit log, I'd flip to a guaranteed pipeline and pay for it."

**🔀 Decision: Pre-aggregate counters vs query-on-read for alerts.**
1. **Chose & why:** stream pre-aggregation — millionfold cheaper than re-scanning logs.
2. **Gave up:** the ability to alert on arbitrary ad-hoc conditions you didn't pre-declare.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like a turnstile counter vs recounting everyone who entered — fast, but you only know the totals you decided to track.
4. **When this reverses:** if teams need fully arbitrary alert expressions, add a (rate-limited) on-demand query path alongside the counters.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Alerts read precomputed counts, so they're cheap; the cost is you declare what to count up front."

**🔀 Decision: Tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) vs all-hot.**
1. **Chose & why:** tiering — $420K vs $2.5M/mo.
2. **Gave up:** instant search over old data (cold needs rehydration).
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like keeping this week's files on your desk and last year's in deep storage you fetch on request.
4. **When this reverses:** if interactive deep-history search becomes common, widen the warm tier (more $).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I tier by age because nobody interactively searches last March; if that changes I grow the warm tier."

**CAP/PACELC:** the ingest/search path is **AP** — under a partition we favor availability (keep ingesting, accept staleness) over consistency; in normal operation (PACELC's "else") we favor **latency** over consistency (5s searchable lag is fine). The control plane (alert rules) is **CP** — we'd rather reject a rule write than persist it inconsistently.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three decisions I'd defend hardest: best-effort sampled ingest, pre-aggregated alerting, and tiered storage. Each trades a guarantee for an order of magnitude of cost or resilience, and each reverses on a clear condition — compliance logs, arbitrary alerts, or heavy deep-history search. Overall the system is AP and latency-favoring on the firehose, and CP on the small config plane."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two services burst at once and you can't index both — who wins?"**
- **Mechanism:** Kafka is partitioned by service, so one noisy service fills its own partitions, not everyone's. The gateway enforces per-service quotas; over quota, that service is sampled first while others stay full-fidelity.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** each service has its own lane and its own speed limit, so one going wild doesn't block the rest.
- 💬 "Per-service partitions plus per-service quotas — one bad deploy can't starve the fleet."

**❓ "What happens when the search index goes down?"**
- **Mechanism:** hot shards have ≥2 replicas; a replica is promoted. If a whole shard is lost, we reindex from the cold archive or replay Kafka. During recovery, dashboards and alerts still work because they read rollups, not the index.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** search blinks, but your alerts and dashboards keep running because they don't depend on the search engine.
- 💬 "Search degrades independently — alerting runs off separate counters, so you're not blind during an index outage."

**❓ "A bad deploy logs at 20x for an hour — does the pipeline survive?"**
- **Mechanism:** Kafka absorbs the initial surge; as indexer lag crosses the ceiling, the gateway sheds debug/info first via head-sampling, keeps all errors, and returns dropped counts so agents back off. Per-service quota caps the offender.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we throw away the chatty noise on purpose and keep the errors, so the pipe bends but doesn't break.
- 💬 "Sustained overload triggers prioritized shedding — never errors, always with honest back-pressure."

**❓ "Can the same log get indexed twice?"**
- **Mechanism:** batches carry a `batch_id` and events an `event_id`; a retried batch is deduped at the gateway (1-hour TTL store), and the indexer uses event_id as the document id so a re-index overwrites rather than duplicates.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** even if an agent retries, we recognize the same batch and don't double-count.
- 💬 "Idempotent on batch_id and event_id — retries are safe, counts stay honest."

**❓ "I just logged an error — why can't I find it immediately?"**
- **Mechanism:** indexing is asynchronous with ~5s lag (read-your-own-writes isn't instant). The UI shows an "indexing…" hint, and a live-tail mode reads directly from Kafka for the truly-now case.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** there's a few-second delay before a log is searchable; live tail skips the index to show it instantly.
- 💬 "Five-second lag by design; live-tail reads the buffer directly for the now."

**❓ "How do you stop an engineer reading another team's logs?"**
- **Mechanism:** identity comes from the JWT; the query service checks the caller's team claims against the target service's owning team (object-level authz) before any index hit. Service ids in the query are never trusted for authz.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we check you actually own the service whose logs you're asking for, not just that you're logged in.
- 💬 "Object-level authz from the JWT — logged-in isn't enough, you must own the service."

**❓ "Could someone forge logs to hide an incident or frame a service?"**
- **Mechanism:** agents authenticate to the gateway; the server stamps `received_at` and validates the source — clients can't set ingestion time. Per-agent rate limits bound abuse. (Full source-spoofing prevention needs mTLS agent certs, which I'd add.)
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** logs are tagged with a server-trusted timestamp and an authenticated agent, so you can't backdate or impersonate easily.
- 💬 "Server-set timestamps + authenticated agents; I'd add mTLS certs to fully lock source identity."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
- **Mechanism:** the ES index fleet (~$600K/mo) is biggest because indexing millions of docs/s burns CPU and NVMe. The top lever is sampling info/debug at source, which cuts index CPU, hot storage, and replicas at once.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the search index costs the most; not indexing the boring logs saves money in three places at once.
- 💬 "Index fleet's the line item; sampling at source is the one cut that hits compute and storage together."

**❓ "Can you oversell — I mean, lose an error you promised to keep?"**
- **Mechanism:** the shedding policy never drops ERROR/FATAL; only debug/info/warn are sampled. Errors also tee uncompressed to cold storage independent of the index, so even an index outage doesn't lose them.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** errors are sacred — we drop chatter, never the things you'd actually investigate.
- 💬 "Errors are never the thing we shed, and they're archived separately as a belt-and-suspenders."

**❓ "How would you add full distributed tracing later?"**
- **Mechanism:** events already carry `trace_id`; tracing is an additive consumer on the same Kafka stream writing to a span store keyed by trace_id, joined to logs in the UI. No change to ingest.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** traces are just another reader of the same firehose, stitched to logs by a shared id.
- 💬 "Tracing is a new consumer on the existing stream — additive, no ingest change."

**❓ "How do you handle late or out-of-order events for alerting?"**
- **Mechanism:** the stream aggregator uses event-time windowing with a small grace period; events arriving within the grace window update their correct minute bucket, very late ones fold into the next bucket and are flagged.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we wait a little before finalizing each minute's count so stragglers still land in the right slot.
- 💬 "Event-time windows with a grace period — late logs still count correctly, within bounds."

**❓ "What if Kafka itself can't keep up at 3 GB/s?"**
- **Mechanism:** ~300 partitions across ~26 brokers spreads load; per-broker sustainable ~120 MB/s. If we exceed that, add brokers/partitions (horizontal). The gateway sheds before overflowing Kafka, so the buffer never becomes the silent-loss point.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** Kafka scales by adding more brokers, and we shed upstream before it ever overflows.
- 💬 "Hundreds of partitions on dozens of brokers; we add brokers to scale and shed before overflow."

**❓ "How would you support multi-region?"**
- **Mechanism:** agents ship to the nearest region's Kafka (locality + resilience); each region indexes locally. Cross-region search fans out to regional query services and merges; alerts evaluate per-region with a global roll-up for company-wide rules.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** each region handles its own firehose, and search stitches regions together when you ask globally.
- 💬 "Ingest is regional for locality; query fans across regions and merges."

**❓ "How do you delete a user's PII from logs for a GDPR request?"**
- **Mechanism:** PII shouldn't be in logs, but for erasure we crypto-shred — logs are written with a per-tenant key, and erasure deletes the key, rendering both the index and cold archive copies unreadable, which also covers the immutable archive.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we encrypt with a key we can throw away, so deleting the key erases the data everywhere at once.
- 💬 "Crypto-shredding — drop the key and the data's gone from index and archive together."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a system that swallows a firehose of logs from a hundred thousand hosts, makes them searchable, and pages people when errors spike. The design splits into two halves. The read half is small — an Elasticsearch-style inverted index, sharded by time so recent searches are sub-second and old data tiers off to cheap storage, fronted by a tiny query service because reads are only about seventy a second. The write half is the whole game — five million events a second through a stateless gateway into Kafka, a durable buffer that absorbs five-x bursts, drained by indexer workers; and the hardest problem there is sustained overload, solved by prioritized sampling that drops debug logs but never errors. The two halves connect through Kafka — the same stream feeds the index, the alerting aggregator, and the cold archive. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: this is write-heavy by seventy thousand to one and allowed to sample, so I spend my entire budget on ingestion durability and storage cost, and I treat dropping the boring logs as a feature, not a failure."
